bins.name: Full cost responsibility for upgrades
bins.desc: Maine OPA strongly supports requiring large loads to bear the full cost of network upgrades they trigger. The filer explicitly opposes any crediting mechanism that would transfer upgrade costs to existing ratepayers.
quotes: the Maine OPA urges the Commission to proceed with caution to ensure that any final rule protects existing ratepayers by requiring large loads to bear the full cost of necessary network upgrades, by respecting existing state jurisdiction over transactions that are fundamentally retail in nature, and by including provisions that minimize any adverse impacts on wholesale market prices and transmission rates from large load interconnection.
load and hybrid facilities should be responsible for 100% of the network upgrades that they are assigned through interconnection studies.

Any crediting mechanism, by necessity, results in the interconnecting entity being relieved of bearing 100% of cost of network upgrades, thus transferring the cost responsibility for the upgrades to other ratepayers.

bins.name: Market price and rate impact mitigation
bins.desc: Maine OPA supports measures to minimize adverse impacts on wholesale market prices and transmission rates. The filer emphasizes that large loads without matching generation can drive up energy and capacity prices, affecting all customers, and urges cautious implementation.
quotes: the Maine OPA urges the Commission to proceed with caution to ensure that any final rule protects existing ratepayers by requiring large loads to bear the full cost of necessary network upgrades, by respecting existing state jurisdiction over transactions that are fundamentally retail in nature, and by including provisions that minimize any adverse impacts on wholesale market prices and transmission rates from large load interconnection.

Absent the corresponding development of supply resources, the presence of new large loads will drive up market clearing prices for both energy and capacity in these wholesale markets, increasing retail prices for other customers.

The process for interconnecting large loads should not be a more accelerated process than that for generators. Doing so would likely trigger price increases for other customers if new loads are not matched with new generation.

Such mechanisms allow these new large loads to minimize their impact on both transmission rates and wholesale market prices, as well as to reduce the cost of interconnection and the ongoing cost of electricity to these customers.

bins.name: Load flexibility and demand response
bins.desc: Maine OPA supports incentivizing large loads to participate in curtailment, load flexibility, and demand response mechanisms as a way to minimize their impact on transmission rates and wholesale prices.
quotes: the Maine OPA also supports the recommendation to allow and/or incentivize large load customers to participate in curtailment, load flexibility, and other demand response mechanisms.

Such mechanisms allow these new large loads to minimize their impact on both transmission rates and wholesale market prices, as well as to reduce the cost of interconnection and the ongoing cost of electricity to these customers.

bins.name: Orderly interconnection process
bins.desc: Maine OPA supports the Secretary's proposal for timely and orderly interconnection reforms and urges that the large load process parallel the generator interconnection timeline to avoid accelerated deployment.
quotes: the Maine OPA supports the Secretary of Energy's proposal to implement reforms to ensure the timely and orderly interconnection of large loads to the interstate transmission system.
any interconnection process adopted by the Commission for large loads should parallel the process applied to interconnecting generators.

bins.name: State jurisdiction over retail transactions
bins.desc: Maine OPA urges FERC to limit its jurisdiction to the interconnection process itself and to respect existing state authority over retail cost recovery, billing, metering, and public policy costs. Maine's experience with large industrial loads treated as retail customers demonstrates this boundary works.
quotes: the Maine OPA urges the Commission to proceed with caution to ensure that any final rule protects existing ratepayers by requiring large loads to bear the full cost of necessary network upgrades, by respecting existing state jurisdiction over transactions that are fundamentally retail in nature, and by including provisions that minimize any adverse impacts on wholesale market prices and transmission rates from large load interconnection.
the Commission should limit the expansion of its jurisdiction relating to these large load customer to interconnection.
These large loads will be end use customers engaging in retail transactions. Such customers are currently and should remain subject to state jurisdiction regarding the recovery of retail costs.

The State of Maine has a number of existing large industrial loads interconnected directly to the high voltage the transmission system. These loads are appropriately treated as retail customers and are required to pay a retail rate.

bins.name: Interconnection cost assignment
bins.desc: Maine OPA supports assigning 100% of network upgrade costs to the large load facilities causing them, rejecting any offsets or credits.
quotes: load and hybrid facilities should be responsible for 100% of the network upgrades that they are assigned through interconnection studies.

bins.name: Interconnection timeline parity
bins.desc: Maine OPA opposes expedited interconnection processes for large loads. The filer warns that accelerating large load interconnection beyond the generator timeline risks price increases if new loads arrive without matching supply.
quotes: any interconnection process adopted by the Commission for large loads should parallel the process applied to interconnecting generators.

The process for interconnecting large loads should not be a more accelerated process than that for generators. Doing so would likely trigger price increases for other customers if new loads are not matched with new generation.

bins.name: ISO New England region
bins.desc: Maine OPA specifically references ISO New England as a restructured market where no entity has direct responsibility to plan and construct generation, making resource adequacy a key concern for large load interconnection.
quotes: The State of Maine has a number of existing large industrial loads interconnected directly to the high voltage the transmission system. These loads are appropriately treated as retail customers and are required to pay a retail rate.
